primary_goal: Build authority for UK ecommerce and wholesale automation through high-quality, specific, operator-led articles. Quality beats quantity; avoid keyword spam and thin SEO pages.
site_structure_note: The public article hub is /articles/. Future posts should use /articles/ unless instructed otherwise.
ai_mention_rule: AI may be covered when it helps the offer: process design, clean data, controls, review rules and ROI proof must come before any tool. Do not frame AI as magic, as a replacement for Henry's work, or as a replacement for the client's judgement.

articles.title: How to catch product data errors before listings go live
articles.topic_type: Commercial operator guide to pre-publish product data controls
articles.primary_keyword_search_query: product data quality checks before publishing ecommerce listings
articles.trend_search_signal: Current search on 17 July 2026 showed active demand around Shopify catalogue trust, missing barcodes and attributes, Amazon listing errors and catalogue conflicts. The article uses that signal without relying on hype or tool promotion.
articles.primary_search_intent: A UK ecommerce operator wants to prevent bad identifiers, prices, variants and missing attributes from reaching Shopify, Amazon Seller Central or eBay.
articles.authority_value: Adds a distinct control-gate topic between supplier-file preparation and channel publishing, with named validation mechanisms, pass-warning-stop rules, exception ownership, pilot testing and payback maths.
articles.duplicate_check: Passed. The supplier article focuses on ingesting and mapping supplier files; the stock-sync article focuses on inventory availability. This article focuses on product-data validation and exception handling before listings go live.
articles.improvement_summary: Expanded the guide to about 1,000 words before FAQs and CTA. Added named checking mechanisms, severity rules, exception ownership, a hard-sample pilot, fresh payback maths, a clear manual-versus-automation decision and stronger operator context.
articles.hemingway_readability_check: Passed. The revised article contains 1,011 words before FAQs and CTA. A local plain-language estimate placed the full article body at Flesch-Kincaid grade 7.1, below the grade 9 ceiling.
articles.credibility_repetition_check: Passed. The article names required-field checks, allowed-value lists, lookup tables, duplicate checks, pattern checks and tolerance formulas. Unsupported channel claims, hype, banned phrases and repeated conclusions were removed.
articles.mobile_layout_check: Passed conceptual checks at about 390px and 768px. The article width is fluid, the two-column check grid collapses to one column, headings use clamp and the CTA becomes full width.
articles.ai_mention_check: Passed. AI is not mentioned because it is not needed for this topic.
articles.search_console_submission: Not submitted; no Search Console tool was available in this runtime.

articles.title: How to stop re-keying wholesale orders into Sage or Xero
articles.topic_type: Commercial operator guide to wholesale order entry automation
articles.primary_keyword_search_query: automate wholesale order entry Sage Xero
articles.trend_search_signal: Lightweight current web search on 10 July 2026 returned weak exact-match coverage but durable buyer intent around repetitive web data entry and ecommerce system integration. The topic was chosen from a clear content-plan gap and direct commercial fit rather than chasing a trend.
articles.primary_search_intent: A UK wholesale or ecommerce operator is re-keying email, PDF or portal orders into Sage, Xero or QuickBooks and wants to know what can be automated without losing price, SKU and customer controls.
articles.authority_value: Builds the missing wholesale and finance admin pillar with a specific source-check-draft-review-post workflow, stop rules, audit trail advice and payback maths.
articles.duplicate_check: Distinct from supplier-file automation, weekly reporting, stock sync, hiring and AI ROI articles. It focuses on inbound wholesale sales orders and the hand-off into finance or ERP systems.
articles.improvement_summary: Expanded the guide to about 1,000 words with named extraction mechanisms, a testable rules sheet, staged draft-to-post rollout, clearer exception handling, fresh payback maths, audit controls and stronger internal links.
articles.hemingway_readability_check: Passed. The revised article is 1,076 words in the article body and the local plain-language estimate is Flesch-Kincaid grade 7.8, below the grade 9 ceiling.
articles.credibility_repetition_check: Passed. Mechanisms are named in plain English; claims are controlled; the worked example uses fresh figures; banned phrases, hype and repeated conclusions were removed.
articles.mobile_layout_check: Passed conceptual checks at about 390px and 768px. Article width is fluid, headings use clamp, cards stack, the CTA is full width on mobile, and no fixed content width can exceed the viewport.
articles.ai_mention_check: Passed. AI is mentioned only in an FAQ to explain that PDF extraction does not automatically require AI; rules, OCR, controls and review remain primary.
articles.search_console_submission: Not submitted; no Search Console tool was available in this runtime.

articles.duplicate_check: Distinct from the hiring article, stock-sync article and supplier-file article. It focuses on ROI proof and governance before choosing tools.
articles.hemingway_readability_check: Passed final plain-language editor check for grade 9 or lower.

quality_rules: Write for UK ecommerce and wholesale operators first, not search engines.
Quality beats quantity; two posts per week is a ceiling, not a quota.
Every article and supporting copy must read at Hemingway grade 9 or lower.
Use current trend and keyword signals to choose useful buyer questions where available, but never keyword-stuff.
AI may be mentioned when it supports the offer: ROI, controls, review rules, process design and practical payback.
Do not frame AI as a replacement for Henry's work or the client's judgement.
No filler, generic automation hype or repeated points.
No keyword spam or pages that exist only to target a phrase.
Do not invent client results, statistics, dates, prices or case studies.
Every article must include a clear decision point, operational example or ROI calculation where relevant.
After the starter articles, use scheduled publishing or refreshes rather than adding volume for its own sake.
